items.prompt: A solution note says: “The algorithm sorts once, scans once, and matches all twelve examples, so the greedy choice is correct.” What is the primary defect?
constraints: The note claims correctness for every legal input.
The examples are not an exhaustive finite domain.

options.text: The note gives efficiency and observations but no argument that the local choice preserves an optimum on every input.
Sorting is too slow for any greedy algorithm.
The proof becomes complete after adding a sufficiently large informal example set.
A method that sorts is not greedy, so the strategy label is wrong.

feedback.reason: Complexity and examples answer different questions from correctness. The missing link is a universal safety argument for the irreversible local choice.
feedback.details:
O(n log n) may be an acceptable cost, and examples may increase confidence or reveal bugs, but neither explains why the algorithm cannot make an irreversible mistake on an untested instance. A greedy proof must connect the chosen local preference to a global optimum, such as by exchange, dominance, a stays-ahead invariant, or induction after a safe first choice.

More non-exhaustive examples never create that quantification. Sorting is compatible with many greedy methods and is not the defect. The review habit is to separate claims: implementation complexity, empirical behavior, feasibility, and optimality each require their own evidence. Tests are powerful for finding counterexamples; they are not a substitute for the correctness contract.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_too_slow: Sorting can be fully acceptable and often defines the greedy processing order. Speed is not the missing correctness argument.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.needs_more_examples_only: Unless the legal domain is exhaustively finite and fully enumerated, additional examples still do not prove the universal claim.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.greedy_cannot_sort: Sort-then-greedy is a standard strategy shape. Greedy refers to the commitment rule, not the absence of preprocessing.

items.prompt: A reviewer writes: “Sort projects by descending reward. The highest reward is obviously the best first choice, so no further justification is needed.” Which diagnosis is most precise?
constraints: Projects can conflict with several other projects.
The objective is total reward of the selected compatible set.

options.text: The claim compares individual rewards but does not show that the first project can replace the compatible set it excludes without losing total value.
Descending sort direction is invalid in every greedy algorithm.
Reward cannot be used as a numeric key.
Calling the choice obvious is a formal proof because the objective also mentions reward.

feedback.reason: The argument ignores opportunity cost and gives no exchange relation. A highest individual reward can block several compatible rewards whose total is larger.
feedback.details:
A sort key must order candidates by a property that controls the residual problem or objective. Descending reward compares each project in isolation. If the first project conflicts with several others, its true consequence includes the value of every combination it removes. The note never proves that replacing those projects with the highest-reward project is no worse.

Descending order is not inherently invalid, and reward can be a legitimate numeric field in problems with the right exchange structure. The defect is the missing structural link, not the data type or direction. The transfer rule is to challenge words such as “obvious,” “natural,” or “largest” by asking what residual boundary the key improves and what optimal exchange it permits.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.descending_never_allowed: Some correct greedy algorithms maximize a key. Direction must be tied to the proof, not banned categorically.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.reward_not_numeric: Reward is numeric here. The problem is not representability but failure to account for interactions among selected projects.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.proof_by_adjective: Restating the objective with an adjective does not show that the local winner is compatible with a global optimum.

items.prompt: A design document says: “The problem has optimal substructure, so greedy and dynamic programming are interchangeable; choose whichever is easier to code.” What is the correct review comment?
constraints: The document has not established a greedy-choice property.
Several partial states may lead to different future values.

options.text: Optimal substructure alone does not justify greedy; greedy also needs a safe local commitment, while dynamic programming may compare multiple states.
Greedy is interchangeable with dynamic programming whenever it has lower running time.
Dynamic programming cannot solve optimization problems.
Any recursive optimal-substructure proof is automatically a greedy proof.

feedback.reason: The methods share neither state retention nor proof obligations. Optimal substructure supports decomposition, but greedy additionally needs a theorem that discarding alternatives is safe.
feedback.details:
Dynamic programming can exploit optimal substructure while retaining several states that encode different histories or resource levels. Greedy commits to one local choice and removes competing possibilities. That stronger discard requires a greedy-choice property, exchange argument, dominance relation, or stays-ahead invariant. Without it, the fact that an optimal solution contains optimal subsolutions does not identify which first choice is safe.

Running time cannot make two incorrect and correct methods interchangeable, and both methods can solve optimization problems. Recursion is an implementation or proof form, not a strategy classification. The transfer rule is to ask what alternatives remain alive after each step. If multiple materially different states must be compared, dynamic programming may fit; if one state dominates all others by proof, greedy may fit.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.greedy_always_faster: Efficiency cannot supply a missing correctness property. A faster wrong algorithm is not interchangeable with a correct state-based one.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.dp_is_not_optimization: Optimization is a primary use of dynamic programming. This option reverses the method’s purpose.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_recursion: A recursive decomposition can still compare multiple choices. Greedy correctness depends on safe commitment, not on whether recursion is present.

items.prompt: An explanation says: “This action leaves the smallest immediate cost. Therefore the complete sequence has minimum total cost.” What is missing?
constraints: Later costs may depend on the current action.
No exchange, dominance, or monotonicity argument has been provided.

options.text: It must show that the lower immediate cost cannot create a worse residual problem than any rejected action.
Nothing is missing; “locally best” is the definition of “globally best.”
Only the time complexity remains to be calculated.
Renaming immediate cost to total cost makes the conclusion valid.

feedback.reason: The conclusion needs a bridge from the current metric to every future consequence. Without residual dominance, a cheap action now can force expensive actions later.
feedback.details:
Total cost decomposes into immediate cost plus the best achievable residual cost. Minimizing only the first term is safe when the chosen action leaves a residual state no worse than the states produced by alternatives, or when an exchange proves that any optimum can use the choice. The explanation provides neither relation. It therefore assumes the exact local-to-global property that needs proof.

Complexity is a separate question, and renaming a quantity cannot change what it measures. The transfer test is to write the objective after the choice: localContribution(choice) + optimalResidual(choice). If the residual term varies and is not dominated by the greedy choice, the immediate comparison is incomplete. A valid greedy proof must control both terms.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.local_equals_global_definition: Greedy is a strategy family, not a definition that makes every local optimum global. The implication is the theorem to prove.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_complexity_missing: An efficient implementation can still return a suboptimal sequence. Correctness must be established before cost analysis closes the argument.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rename_cost: Changing terminology does not include future costs or prove that the residual state is dominated.

items.prompt: An author claims: “Sort intervals by earliest start and keep each non-overlapping interval. This is optimal because it starts work as soon as possible; several examples pass. Ties and touching endpoints need no definition.” Which review findings are valid? Select all statements that apply.
constraints: The objective is maximum selected count.
The interval endpoint model has not been specified elsewhere.

options.text: Earliest start does not control when the resource becomes free; a long early interval can block a larger compatible set.
Passing examples do not establish that an optimum can exchange its first interval for the proposed choice.
Whether touching intervals are compatible changes the acceptance inequality and must be stated.
Any algorithm that sorts must be replaced by dynamic programming.
No interval problem can have a correct greedy solution.

feedback.reason: The proposal has three independent defects: the key optimizes the wrong boundary, examples do not prove safety, and compatibility is ambiguous without endpoint semantics.
feedback.details:
Maximum-cardinality interval selection is governed by the end of the selected interval because that end determines which future intervals remain feasible. Earliest start can select a long interval that blocks several later intervals. Even if examples happen to avoid that case, they do not show that every optimum can be transformed to begin with the earliest-start choice. Finally, “non-overlapping” is incomplete until touching endpoints are classified, because the scan must choose between >= and > at the boundary.

Sorting itself is compatible with a correct earliest-finish greedy algorithm, and interval problems include both greedy-solvable and non-greedy variants. The review transfer template is: verify the local key controls the decisive residual state, require universal safety evidence, and surface all assumptions that alter feasibility before the learner answers.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_requires_dp: Sorting is a preprocessing step used by many correct greedy algorithms. The defect is the unsupported key, not sorting itself.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.greedy_never_optimal: Unweighted maximum-cardinality interval selection has a correct earliest-finish greedy solution. The family cannot be rejected categorically.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.start_not_future_boundary: Without this finding, the review misses the central strategy error: start time is not the residual compatibility boundary.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.examples_not_exchange: Without this finding, empirical agreement is incorrectly treated as a universal optimality proof.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.endpoint_contract_missing: Without this finding, the implementation and accepted schedule remain ambiguous for intervals that touch.
